title: Sampling techniques and data collection
summary: Every statistical study begins with a decision that quietly determines whether its conclusions are worth anything: who, or what, do we actually measure? This lesson builds the full 4.1 syllabus on data collection — the difference between a population and a sample and why we sample at all; the five sampling methods you must be able to name, apply and criticise (simple random, systematic, stratified, quota and convenience); how bias creeps in and undermines reliability; the classification of data as qualitative or quantitative, and quantitative data as discrete or continuous; and what it means for a data-collection process to be reliable and valid. Because 4.1 is examined on Paper 2, the marks reward clear method and reasoning in context — so the stratified-sampling worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks, method mark by accuracy mark.
learningObjectives: Distinguish a population from a sample, explain why sampling is used instead of a census, and classify data as qualitative or quantitative (discrete or continuous).
Describe and apply the five required sampling methods — simple random, systematic, stratified, quota and convenience — and state a clear advantage and limitation of each.
Calculate the number to take from each group in a stratified sample using proportional allocation.
Identify sources of bias in a sampling design, explain in context how they threaten reliability, and comment on the reliability and validity of a data-collection method.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Tasting the Soup
simpleExplanation.summary: We usually want to know something about a huge group — the population — but measuring every member is impossible or wasteful. So we measure a small, carefully chosen subset — a sample — and trust it to speak for the whole. The entire art is choosing the sample so that it genuinely represents the population rather than some corner of it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a large pot of soup. To judge the seasoning you do not drink the whole pot; you stir it well and taste one spoonful. Stirring is the randomising step: it makes your single spoonful represent the whole pot. Skip the stir and scoop only from the top, and you might catch all the floating herbs and wrongly declare the soup over-seasoned. A biased sample is an unstirred spoonful — small, convenient, and quietly misleading.
steps: Define the population precisely and decide exactly what data you need to collect from each member.
Choose a sampling method that fits the population and your resources, and that keeps the sample representative.
Carry out the plan and record the data, using a sampling frame (a list of the population) where the method needs one.
Interpret the results, but also state the limitations and any bias your method may have introduced — a good answer criticises its own data.

flashcards.back: The population is the entire group you want to know about (e.g. all students in a school). A sample is a subset of that population, chosen to be studied so that conclusions can be generalised back to the whole. A study of the whole population is a census.

flashcards.front: Why sample instead of a census?
flashcards.back: A census measures everyone, so it is accurate but usually too slow, too expensive, or physically impossible (you cannot weigh every fish in a lake). A well-designed sample gives a reliable estimate for a fraction of the cost and time.

A list of every member of the population, used to select the sample. Simple random and systematic sampling need one; if the frame is incomplete or out of date, bias enters before a single value is collected.

Every member of the population has an equal, independent chance of selection — like drawing numbered names from a hat, usually via a random number generator. Unbiased in principle, but needs a full sampling frame and may under-represent small subgroups by chance.

Order the population, choose a random start, then take every $k^{\text{th}}$ member, where $k = \dfrac{\text{population size}}{\text{sample size}}$. Quick and even in coverage, but biased if the list has a repeating pattern that lines up with $k$.

Split the population into non-overlapping strata (e.g. year groups), then take a simple random sample from each stratum, with each stratum's share of the sample proportional to its share of the population. Guarantees every subgroup is represented in the right proportion.

Like stratified sampling — the population is split into groups and a fixed number is required from each — but selection within a group is NON-random: the interviewer simply finds people who fit. Fast and needs no frame, but the interviewer's choices introduce selection bias.

Select whoever is easiest to reach — friends, passers-by, the first 30 people in the canteen. The fastest and cheapest method, and the most biased: the sample reflects who was convenient, not the population.

Number taken from a stratum $= \dfrac{\text{stratum size}}{\text{population size}} \times \text{sample size}$. Round each to the nearest whole number and adjust so the parts add up to the required total.

A systematic tendency for a sample to over- or under-represent parts of the population, so estimates are consistently off in one direction. It cannot be fixed by taking a larger sample of the same flawed kind — a bigger biased sample is just a bigger mistake.
Qualitative (categorical) data describes a quality in words or categories (eye colour, nationality, brand). Quantitative data is numerical and measured or counted (height, number of siblings).

Discrete quantitative data can take only separate, countable values, usually whole numbers (number of cars, goals scored). Continuous quantitative data can take any value in a range and is measured (height, time, mass) — limited only by the precision of the instrument.

A method is reliable if it gives consistent results when repeated under the same conditions. It is valid if it actually measures what it claims to. A method can be reliable but not valid — a wrongly-calibrated scale gives the same wrong weight every time.

flashcards.front: Stratified vs quota — the key difference
flashcards.back: Both split the population into groups with proportional targets. In stratified sampling the members of each group are chosen randomly (a probability method); in quota sampling they are chosen non-randomly by the interviewer, which is why quota sampling is prone to bias.

quickCheck.prompt: A town has 3000 adults and 2000 children. A stratified sample of 100 people is required. How many children should be in the sample?
In systematic sampling, after a random starting point you select:
options: Every $k^{\text{th}}$ member of an ordered list
Members entirely at random from a hat
Whoever is most convenient to reach
A fixed quota from each subgroup, chosen by the interviewer
quickCheck.answer: Every $k^{\text{th}}$ member of an ordered list

quickCheck.prompt: A student surveys only their own friends about screen time. The main problem with this sample is that it is:
options: A convenience sample and therefore likely biased and unrepresentative
A stratified sample with equal strata
quickCheck.answer: A convenience sample and therefore likely biased and unrepresentative

quickCheck.prompt: Which of the following is continuous quantitative data?
options: The exact time, in seconds, each runner takes to finish a race
The number of students in each class
The favourite sport of each student
The number of goals scored in a match
quickCheck.answer: The exact time, in seconds, each runner takes to finish a race

quickCheck.prompt: The essential difference between stratified and quota sampling is that stratified sampling:
options: Selects members within each group at random, whereas quota sampling does not
Uses larger samples than quota sampling
Does not divide the population into groups
Is a non-probability method, unlike quota sampling
quickCheck.answer: Selects members within each group at random, whereas quota sampling does not

faq.q: Is simple random sampling always the best method?
faq.a: No. It is unbiased in theory, but it needs a complete list of the population, and it can be impractical when the population is huge or spread out. By chance it may also under-represent a small but important subgroup. When you need to guarantee that specific groups appear in the right proportion, stratified sampling is usually the better choice.

faq.q: What is the real difference between a stratum and a quota?
faq.a: Both split the population into groups with a target number from each. The difference is how members are chosen. Stratified sampling takes a random sample within each stratum, so it is a probability method. Quota sampling lets the interviewer pick anyone who fits the quota — a non-random choice that tends to favour approachable, available people and so introduces bias.

faq.q: My stratified sample sizes don't add up to the total after rounding. What do I do?
faq.a: This is common. Round each stratum's figure to the nearest whole number, then check the sum. If it is one out, adjust the largest group up or down by one so the parts add to the required total, and say that you have done so. For example, if you need 100 and your rounded parts give 99, add one to the biggest stratum.

faq.q: Why does the IB care so much about bias?
faq.a: Because bias defeats the whole point of sampling. If the sample systematically misrepresents the population, no amount of neat calculation afterwards can rescue the conclusion — and a bigger sample of the same biased kind does not help. In polling, medical trials and market research, biased data leads to confidently wrong decisions, so the IB wants you to evaluate a data-collection method critically, not just compute from it.

faq.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
faq.a: Reliability is about consistency: repeat the method under the same conditions and you get the same result. Validity is about accuracy of purpose: the method actually measures what it claims to. They are independent. A miscalibrated scale is reliable (same reading every time) but not valid (the readings are wrong). A good data-collection method needs both.

sections.content: How does a government decide policy, a company launch a product, or a scientist judge the health of a river? Each starts by collecting data — and the quality of every conclusion is capped by the quality of that first step. This lesson covers the full 4.1 syllabus on data collection: populations and samples, why we sample, the five sampling methods you must be able to name and criticise, the sources of bias that quietly wreck a study, the classification of data, and what makes a method reliable and valid. Because 4.1 is examined on Paper 2, the marks reward clear method and reasoning in context — so, as in every calculation topic, the worked examples below are laid out exactly the way our engine marks your own practice.
Populations, samples, and why we sample

In any statistical investigation the population is the entire group you want to know something about — 'all students at this school', 'every apple in the orchard', 'all the fish in the lake'. Measuring the whole population is a census: perfectly accurate in principle, but usually too slow, too expensive, or physically impossible. Instead you study a sample, a subset chosen to stand in for the population, and generalise your findings back to the whole. The quantity you calculate from the population (such as its true mean) is a parameter; the quantity you calculate from the sample and use to estimate it is a statistic.

items: **Population:** the complete set of individuals or items under study.
**Sample:** a subset of the population, selected to be measured.
**Census:** a study of every member of the population — accurate but often impractical.
**Sampling frame:** a list of all members of the population, from which a sample is drawn. Some methods need one; a poor frame biases the sample before any data is collected.

sections.content: We sample because it is faster, cheaper, and sometimes the only option: testing every match in a factory by striking it would leave nothing to sell. A good sample is representative — its structure mirrors the population's — so the trade for that saving is small. A bad sample distorts the population, and then even flawless arithmetic afterwards yields a wrong answer.
Types of data

Before choosing how to collect data, classify what you are collecting, because it shapes every later choice of graph and statistic. Data is first split into qualitative and quantitative, and quantitative data is then split into discrete and continuous.

items: **Qualitative (categorical) data:** describes a quality in words or categories — nationality, eye colour, favourite sport. It has no numerical value you can average.
**Quantitative data:** numerical data that is counted or measured — heights, temperatures, number of pets.
**Discrete data:** quantitative data taking only separate, countable values, typically whole numbers — number of siblings, goals scored. You cannot score 2.5 goals.
**Continuous data:** quantitative data taking any value within a range, obtained by measuring — height, mass, time. Its precision is limited only by the measuring instrument.

sections.content: A quick test for discrete versus continuous: ask whether values between two neighbours can occur. Between 2 and 3 children there is nothing — discrete. Between 2 m and 3 m there are infinitely many heights — continuous. Money is a common grey area: strictly it comes in fixed steps (cents), so it is discrete, but with large amounts it is often treated as continuous.
The five sampling methods

The way you choose a sample decides whether it represents the population. You must be able to describe five methods, apply them, and give an advantage and a limitation of each. The first three are probability (random) methods, in which selection is governed by chance; the last two are non-probability methods, in which human choice enters — and with it, the risk of bias.

items: **Simple random sampling:** every member has an equal, independent chance of selection — numbers drawn by a random number generator. *Advantage:* unbiased and simple to justify. *Limitation:* needs a full sampling frame and can miss small subgroups by chance.

**Systematic sampling:** order the population, pick a random start, then take every $k^{\text{th}}$ member with $k = \frac{\text{population size}}{\text{sample size}}$. *Advantage:* fast and spreads the sample evenly across the list. *Limitation:* biased if the list has a repeating pattern matching $k$.

**Stratified sampling:** divide the population into strata (e.g. year groups), then randomly sample each stratum in proportion to its size. *Advantage:* guarantees every subgroup is represented in the correct proportion. *Limitation:* needs known strata sizes and more organisation.

**Quota sampling:** set a target number from each group, but let the interviewer pick who fills it, non-randomly. *Advantage:* fast, cheap, needs no sampling frame. *Limitation:* the interviewer's choices cause selection bias.
**Convenience sampling:** take whoever is easiest to reach. *Advantage:* the quickest and cheapest of all. *Limitation:* almost always biased and unrepresentative.

sections.content: Learn the two families. Simple random, systematic and stratified are random (probability) methods; quota and convenience are non-random. Stratified and quota look alike on the page — both split the population into groups with proportional targets — but stratified samples each group at random while quota does not. That single word, 'random', is what separates a defensible method from a biased one, and examiners test it constantly.
Stratified sampling: proportional allocation

Stratified sampling is the one method that reliably produces calculation marks, so it deserves a clean routine. The idea: each stratum should contribute the same fraction of the sample as it makes up of the population. The number taken from a stratum is therefore its share of the population multiplied by the total sample size.

Number from a stratum $= \dfrac{\text{stratum size}}{\text{population size}} \times \text{total sample size}$. \n Round each result to the nearest whole number, then adjust the largest stratum if necessary so the parts sum to the required total.

sections.question:
A school has 1200 students. The head wants to survey a stratified sample of 80 students about a new uniform policy. There are 550 students in the Middle Years Programme (MYP) and 650 in the Diploma Programme (DP). 
 
 (a) State the population and the sample size. 
 (b) Calculate how many MYP and how many DP students should be surveyed. 
 (c) Describe how the DP students for the sample could then be chosen. [6]
sections.solution:
**(a)** The population is all 1200 students at the school; the sample size is 80.  [A1][A1]

**(b)** Each stratum's share of the sample equals its share of the population, so multiply each stratum size by $\dfrac{80}{1200}$ (the sampling fraction).  [M1: proportional method]
MYP: $\dfrac{550}{1200} 	imes 80 = 36.66\ldots \approx 37$ students.  [A1]
DP: $\dfrac{650}{1200} 	imes 80 = 43.33\ldots \approx 43$ students.  [A1]
Check the parts add to the total: $37 + 43 = 80$. ✓

**(c)** Take a simple random sample of the 43 DP students: obtain a list of all 650 DP students, number them $1$ to $650$, and use a random number generator to pick 43 distinct numbers; the corresponding students are surveyed.  [A1]

Random selection within the stratum is what keeps this a probability method rather than a quota.

sections.content: Bias and reliability

Bias is a systematic tendency for a sampling method to over- or under-represent parts of the population, so estimates are consistently wrong in one direction. It is the central danger of data collection, and the reason the IB rewards criticism as much as calculation. Crucially, bias is not cured by a bigger sample — a larger convenience sample simply repeats the same distortion on a grander scale. It is cured by a better method.

items: **Selection bias:** the method itself favours certain members — surveying transport habits only at a train station misses everyone who drives, cycles or walks.
**Non-response bias:** those who choose not to reply differ systematically from those who do, so the responders misrepresent the whole.
**Convenience and quota methods** are especially prone to bias, because who ends up in the sample depends on who was easy or willing to include.

sections.content: Reliability and validity are the two lenses for judging a data-collection method. A method is reliable if repeating it under the same conditions gives consistent results, and valid if it genuinely measures what it claims to. They are independent qualities: a miscalibrated scale is reliable but not valid, giving the same wrong mass every time. Biased sampling threatens both — it makes results systematically inaccurate (a validity failure) and, if the source of bias shifts between studies, inconsistent too.

Paper 2 questions ask you to 'justify' a sampling choice or 'discuss' bias, and marks are lost by answering in the abstract. Never write only 'convenience sampling is biased'. Name the specific group that is over- or under-represented in this scenario and say which way the estimate is pushed — for example, 'sampling only gym members overstates average exercise, because the least active people are absent from the frame'. Context is where the mark lives.

sections.question:
An environmental scientist wants to estimate the mean number of plastic bottles per 100 m stretch along a 5 km riverbank. She walks to the 100 m stretch nearest her office, counts the bottles there, and uses that count as her estimate for the whole 5 km. 
 
 (a) Name the sampling method used. 
 (b) Explain, in context, why this may give a biased estimate, and suggest a better method. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a)** She chose the stretch that was easiest to reach, so this is convenience sampling.  [A1]

**(b)** The stretch beside an office is unlikely to represent the whole 5 km: it might be cleaner because of regular maintenance, or dirtier because of heavy footfall, so a single count near her office systematically mis-estimates the riverbank as a whole.  [R1: bias identified in context]
A better approach is systematic sampling: divide the 5000 m bank into fifty 100 m stretches, choose a random starting stretch, then sample every fifth stretch (say the 3rd, 8th, 13th, ...) so the sample is spread along the entire length.  [M1: valid method]  [A1: clear description]
Simple random sampling of the fifty stretches would work equally well; the essential fix is that the stretches are chosen by chance, not by convenience.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Allocating a stratified sample equally instead of proportionally** — a stratum with twice the members should contribute twice as many to the sample. Use $\frac{\text{stratum size}}{\text{population size}} \times \text{sample size}$, not the sample split evenly across groups.
**Rounded stratum sizes that do not add up to the total** — round each part, then adjust the largest stratum by one so the pieces sum to the required sample size, and say you have done so.
**Confusing stratified with quota sampling** — both use proportional groups, but stratified samples each group at random while quota does not. Only stratified is a probability method.

**Describing systematic sampling without the random start** — you must pick a random starting point and then take every $k^{\text{th}}$ member; taking 'every $k^{\text{th}}$' from a fixed start is incomplete.
**Criticising bias in the abstract** — 'convenience sampling is biased' earns little. Name who is over- or under-represented in the given scenario and which way the estimate is pushed.
**Thinking a bigger sample removes bias** — increasing the size of a biased sample does not make it representative; only changing the method does.
**Misclassifying data** — height and time are continuous (measured), while counts such as number of siblings or goals are discrete; favourite colour or nationality is qualitative, not quantitative.
**Confusing reliability with validity** — reliable means consistent on repetition; valid means it measures the right thing. A method can be one without the other.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

In Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working — a method mark (M) or an accuracy mark (A) — and an accuracy mark depends on the method mark it follows. Follow-through (FT) means an earlier slip need not cost you the marks that depend on it, provided the later step is carried out correctly on your own figures. The engine also accepts any equivalent form and any correctly-rounded value. But that protection only exists if the method is on the page. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: A school has 400 Year 12 and 600 Year 13 students. A stratified sample of 50 is taken. How many should be from each year group? [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

The total population is $400 + 600 = 1000$ students, and the sampling fraction is $\dfrac{50}{1000}$. Each year group contributes this same fraction of its members:

Year 12: $\dfrac{400}{1000} 	imes 50 = 20$ students.

Year 13: $\dfrac{600}{1000} 	imes 50 = 30$ students.

Check: $20 + 30 = 50$, the required sample size. ✓

**Answer:** 20 from Year 12 and 30 from Year 13.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks:**

- **M1 — correct proportion method.** A method mark for setting up the proportional allocation — multiplying a year group's share of the population by the sample size, e.g. $ rac{400}{1000} 	imes 50$ (equivalently $ rac{50}{1000} 	imes 400$). It is the method that is rewarded, so it stands even if the arithmetic that follows slips.
- **A1 — 20 (Year 12).** An accuracy mark for the correct Year 12 figure of 20, dependent on the M1 method above.
- **A1 — 30 (Year 13).** An accuracy mark for the correct Year 13 figure of 30. This is FT on the method: a candidate who found Year 12 and then used $50 - 20$, or who reapplied the proportion, earns it for a correct value on their own working.

**'Accept equivalent forms and correct rounding.'** The engine accepts the proportion written as $ rac{400}{1000}	imes 50$, as $0.4 	imes 50$, or as $ rac{2}{5}	imes 50$, and accepts the answers whether given as '20 and 30' or 'Year 12: 20, Year 13: 30'. Once the two correct values appear, later restatement does not lose marks.

**Bottom line:** the single method mark is the load-bearing line — a candidate who just writes '20 and 30' with no proportion shown risks the M1, and with it the follow-through that shields the accuracy marks. Show the fraction, and the method protects your score even if a number slips.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with method marks, dependent accuracy marks and follow-through. So always show the method: write the total population, form the sampling fraction, multiply it by each group's size, and check the parts add to the sample total. The method mark and follow-through are what protect your score when a number slips — and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

Data collection is the foundation the rest of the statistics course is built on. The sample you gather here becomes the raw material of descriptive statistics — the means, medians, quartiles and standard deviations of the next lesson — and the honesty of every one of those summaries is inherited from the honesty of your sampling. Later, the idea that a sample estimates a population parameter grows into confidence and hypothesis testing, where representative sampling is the assumption that makes the whole theory valid. Get the first step right — a representative sample, a clear method, and an open acknowledgement of bias — and everything that follows rests on solid ground.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 sampling question marked: calculate a stratified sample and justify a method with full working
